title: CCPA/CPRA — Cross-Context Behavioral Advertising — Cross-Site ID Sync Pixel After GPC Signal

compliance.assurance_rationale: CCPA/CPRA §1798.135 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.135 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.4.21: CCPA/CPRA — Cross-Context Behavioral Advertising — Cross-Site ID Sync Pixel After GPC Signal.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.4.21' running on sourcetype cmp:gpc and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Server-side advertising telemetry HEC, CDN logs (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=web sourcetype="cmp:gpc" gpc=1 earliest=-7d
| eval gpc_time=_time
| join type=left session_id [
    search index=web sourcetype="ads:sync" cross_context_flag=true earliest=-7d
    | fields session_id, partner_id, _time
  ]
| where _time>=gpc_time
| stats count by partner_id, session_id
| sort - count
description: Global Privacy Control obligates downstream behavior for covered businesses. Detecting third-party sync pixels or bid requests carrying cross-context identifiers after a GPC-positive session start evidences broken CMP / tag enforcement for “sale/share” pathways.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for CCPA/CPRA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes web and sourcetypes ads:sync, cmp:gpc. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ensure `gpc_time` uses same clock as ad logs; (2) maintain list of `partner_id` considered cross-context; (3) integrate with tag monitoring remediation; (4) legal review for non-California visitors via geo enrichment optional; (5) document CMP version in release notes. Indexes required: `index=web`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=ads:sync`, `sourcetype=cmp:gpc`.

references.title: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) — Full Text

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorized privacy team operations** — The privacy operations team performs routine data subject request processing, deletion verifications, and privacy impact assessments that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These are documented in the approved CCPA workflow procedures and should be excluded by the privacy-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled data retention processing** — Automated data lifecycle management jobs execute scheduled deletions and anonymisation runs as part of the approved data retention policy. These produce expected data processing signals that are pre-approved by the Chief Privacy Officer.

3. **Third-party service provider integrations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate data processing under written agreements (CCPA service provider contracts) generate cross-boundary data flows that may trigger detection rules during normal operations.

4. **Consumer-initiated account management** — Consumers exercising self-service account preferences, privacy settings changes, and opt-out selections through the official web portal create processing events that align with normal business operations under the CCPA framework.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `ccpa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved service accounts, scheduled job identifiers, and known service provider IP ranges. The correlation search applies a left join against this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `web` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§1798.135`. The control objective is: "Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.135 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.4.21: CCPA/CPRA — Cross-Context Behavioral Advertising — Cross-Site ID Sync Pixel After GPC Signal." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `web` under `cmp:gpc`, `ads:sync`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Ensure `gpc_time` uses same clock as ad logs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** maintain list of `partner_id` considered cross-context. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** integrate with tag monitoring remediation. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 4:** legal review for non-California visitors via geo enrichment optional. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** document CMP version in release notes. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "CCPA/CPRA — Cross-Context Behavioral Advertising — Cross-Site ID Sync Pixel After GPC Signal," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the CCPA/CPRA control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`web`, sourcetype=`cmp:gpc`) with a subsearch on `session_id` across the last 7 days. `type=left` preserves all rows from the outer search; nulls in the inner side indicate missing data that may itself constitute a finding.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.4.21: CCPA/CPRA — Cross-Context Behavioral Advertising — Cross-Sit]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.4.21,reg=CCPA/CPRA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=web sourcetype=cmp:gpc earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `gpc_time`, `partner_id`, `session_id`. Run:

```spl
index=web sourcetype=cmp:gpc earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table gpc_time, partner_id, session_id
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.4.21: CCPA/CPRA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`partner_id`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Distribution (the key fields):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`sessions`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'CCPA/CPRA current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**CCPA/CPRA-specific failure modes:**

• Consumer Request categories: CCPA distinguishes Right to Know, Right to Delete, Right to Opt-Out, Right to Correct, Right to Limit. Detections on 'consumer request' must specify category — the 45-day clock applies differently to each.

• Verifiable Consumer Requests: the consumer identity must be verified 'to a reasonable degree.' Detections measuring request-to-response latency must start the clock at verification complete, not at request receipt — this is a common source of false SLA failures.

• Sale vs share distinction: CPRA broadens 'sale' to include 'share' for cross-context behavioural advertising. Detections on consent-to-share must use the current law wording; a CCPA-only detection misses the CPRA expansion.

• Do Not Sell / Do Not Share signal (GPC): Global Privacy Control must be honoured. Detections must treat GPC-asserting browsers as opted-out irrespective of UI consent state — the technical signal is authoritative.
grandmaExplanation: We global Privacy Control obligates downstream behavior for covered businesses.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'CCPA/CPRA — Cross-Context Behavioral Advertising — Cross-Sit' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper CCPA/CPRA control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the CCPA/CPRA control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with CCPA/CPRA control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the CCPA/CPRA programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
